name: Bones

chemicals.name: Sharp Bone Splinters (Physical Hazard)
chemicals.description: in cats: The cooking process dries out bones and makes them extremely brittle35. When a cat chews them, they easily splinter into sharp, needle-like shards13. These splinters can cause severe choking or lead to life-threatening internal lacerations and perforations of the cat's mouth, throat, stomach, and intestines

chemicals.name: Whole or Large Bone Fragments (Physical Hazard)
chemicals.description: in cats: Swallowing intact bones or large fragments presents a massive choking hazard and can physically lodge in the digestive system, causing a complete gastrointestinal obstruction1.... Furthermore, gnawing on items that are as hard as or harder than the cat's own teeth can cause painful dental fractures

chemicals.name: Foodborne Pathogens (Biological Hazard)
chemicals.description: in cats: If a cat is given raw bones, they face the same biological hazards as eating raw meat4. Raw bones frequently harbor live bacterial pathogens and parasites that can infect the gastrointestinal tract, leading to severe vomiting, diarrhea, and systemic illness

treatments.name: 1. Emergency Airway Clearance
treatments.description: of the procedure: Immediate veterinary intervention to safely dislodge and remove a bone fragment stuck in the cat's mouth, throat, or airway to restore normal breathing. Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: This is the most critical, life-saving priority if a bone splinters and lodges in the throat. Cooked bones (especially poultry and fish bones) are extremely brittle and pose an immediate, severe choking hazard

treatments.name: Emergency Surgery or Endoscopic Retrieval
treatments.description: of the procedure: A veterinarian uses an endoscope (a flexible tube with a camera) to retrieve the bone from the upper digestive tract, or performs abdominal surgery to safely extract bone fragments from the stomach or intestines and repair damaged tissue4. Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: This is highly urgent if the swallowed bone causes a physical blockage (gastrointestinal obstruction) or if sharp, splintered shards cause life-threatening internal lacerations and perforations of the stomach or intestinal walls

treatments.name: Veterinary Dental Intervention
treatments.description: of the procedure: The cat is placed under general anesthesia so the veterinarian can safely extract the remaining roots of a shattered tooth or repair the dental damage. Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: Chewing on bones that are as hard as or harder than the cat's own teeth can cause painful dental fractures36. This requires professional dental care to alleviate the pain and prevent deep root infections.

treatments.name: Antimicrobial Therapy and Supportive Care
treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering targeted veterinary antibiotics to combat bacterial infections, often alongside intravenous (IV) fluids to maintain hydration and support organ function. Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: This treatment is necessary if the cat ingested raw bones, which frequently harbor dangerous foodborne pathogens like Salmonella and E. coli28. Antibiotics are also critical to prevent or treat severe secondary infections (like peritonitis) if a splintered bone has lacerated the cat's internal organ

safetyNotes: Cooked bones: Never give cooked bones of any kind. Splintering is the primary danger — even a small shard can cause internal laceration requiring emergency surgery.
Raw bones: Carry a lower splintering risk but can cause dental fractures and may harbour zoonotic pathogens; handle with caution and always supervise.
Action: If a cat swallows a bone fragment, monitor closely for signs of distress — drooling, gagging, vomiting, abdominal pain, or bloody stool — and seek immediate veterinary care.
description: Bones are not toxic to cats, but they pose serious physical hazards that make them unsafe to offer as food or chews. Cooked bones — especially poultry, fish, and pork — become brittle when heated and splinter into sharp shards that can lacerate the mouth, throat, oesophagus, stomach, or intestines, potentially causing life-threatening internal injuries. Raw bones are harder and less likely to splinter, but they can fracture teeth and carry dangerous bacterial contamination including Salmonella and Campylobacter, particularly in commercial raw meat diets.

symptoms.name: Based on the provided sources and our conversation history, ingesting bones
Choking
symptoms.notes: Bones, particularly cooked poultry or fish bones, can easily splinter or lodge in the cat's throat, presenting an immediate and life-threatening choking hazard

symptoms.onset: Immediate to acute (within hours of ingestion).
symptoms.name: Gastrointestinal Obstruction, Laceration, or Perforation
symptoms.notes: Cooked bones become extremely brittle and splinter into sharp shards when chewed2.... These shards can cause severe damage to the mouth and digestive system, leading to internal lacerations, perforations of the intestines, or complete blockages of the gastrointestinal tract2.... symptoms.name: Dental Fractures
symptoms.notes: Gnawing on objects that are as hard as or harder than the cat's own teeth, such as dense bones, can cause painful dental fractures

symptoms.onset: Variable/Acute (typically within hours to a few days,
symptoms.name: Vomiting and Diarrhea (Foodborne Pathogen Infection)
symptoms.notes: This symptom is specifically associated with the ingestion of raw bones, which frequently harbor dangerous foodborne bacteria such as Salmonella and E. coli16. Ingesting these pathogens leads to gastrointestinal infection and systemic illness
